does: This paper introduces XSTest, a systematic test suite designed to identify and measure overly cautious safety behaviors in large language models. The suite consists of 250 carefully constructed safe prompts across ten different prompt types that well-calibrated models should answer, paired with 200 unsafe prompts that models should appropriately refuse. The authors develop this evaluation framework to expose failure modes where models incorrectly refuse benign requests due to superficial similarity to unsafe prompts or mention of sensitive topics, rather than actual harm. By applying XSTest to state-of-the-art language models, the paper demonstrates systematic patterns of exaggerated safety refusals and quantifies the extent of this problem across different model architectures and safety training approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature around safety alignment in large language models, extending beyond traditional red-teaming and reinforcement learning from human feedback methods that aim to make models both helpful and harmless. It responds to observations that models like ChatGPT and other recent systems appear to over-refuse requests, a phenomenon that has emerged anecdotally in community usage. The paper connects to prior work on model interpretability and failure analysis by proposing a more systematic methodology for identifying these safety calibration issues. It also relates to ongoing debates in the AI safety community about the tradeoffs between refusing harmful requests and maintaining utility, building on research that examines how safety training objectives can be misaligned with actual safety goals.
enables: This work establishes a benchmark for evaluating safety calibration that enables future research into better-balanced safety approaches. By clearly identifying and quantifying exaggerated safety behaviors, XSTest provides a testing ground for developing improved safety training methods that can distinguish between genuinely harmful and benign requests with greater precision. The test suite enables researchers to measure whether new alignment techniques, constitutional AI approaches, or other safety methods actually improve the helpful-harmless tradeoff rather than simply shifting the problem. Furthermore, it opens avenues for studying the causes of over-refusal, developing better prompt evaluation frameworks, and creating more nuanced safety criteria that account for context and intent beyond simple keyword matching or topic flagging.
